The Netflix show Addolences is yet another example of how far Western culture has drifted from reality, responsibility, and reason. Instead of portraying meaningful growth or authentic struggles, it glamorizes dysfunctional behaviour and disoriented values. This is a clear symptom of a civilization that's losing its grounding in tradition, accountability, and societal cohesion.

What’s most troubling is how these kinds of fictional attitudes—over-the-top emotional drama, hyper-individualism, and disregard for any sense of cultural continuity—are increasingly becoming normalized, not just on screen but in real life, particularly in the West. It’s hard to imagine such storylines being taken seriously in many non-Western societies, where family, honor, and discipline still hold weight. Instead, Addolences pushes a narrative that seems almost alien to much of the rest of the world—one where personal gratification trumps collective well-being, and identity is built on rebellion rather than roots.

Western media, once a leader in storytelling and innovation, now often seems trapped in a feedback loop of self-deprecation, nihilism, and moral ambiguity. If Addolences is any reflection of what’s considered “relatable” or “real” in modern Western youth culture, it’s a grim forecast for the future of that society.

Secular humanism is a philosophy, belief system, or life stance that embraces human reason, logic, secular ethics, and philosophical naturalism, while specifically rejecting religious dogma, supernaturalism, and superstition as the basis of morality and decision making.

Secular humanism posits that human beings are capable of being ethical and moral without religion or belief in a deity. It does not, however, assume that humans are either inherently good or evil, nor does it present humans as being superior to nature. Rather, the humanist life stance emphasizes the unique responsibility facing humanity and the ethical consequences of human decisions. Fundamental to the concept of secular humanism is the strongly held viewpoint that ideology be it religious or political must be thoroughly examined by each individual and not simply accepted or rejected on faith. Along with this, an essential part of secular humanism is a continually adapting search for truth, primarily through science and philosophy.

People like Andrew Tate didn't create a problem, they merely capitalized on an already existing one. Why is it always social media that is blamed? For a start, there is a lack of places specifically for kids and that kids can afford anymore. No youth clubs or youth centres for kids to go and socialize at after school, many schools don't have a lot of extra-curriculars. Things like the cinema or paid extracurriculars cost too much particularly for working class children. You have a lot of kids whose families have been financially squeezed out of being able to provide them a life, and wonder why they are angry and discotentful? Neo-Liberal hyper-individualism and me, me, me ideology means many people just cant look eye to eye anymore. Kids have no accountability in school because their parents never moved past adolescent feelings of blaming everyone but themselves, which leads to a behaviour crisis in schools.

Secular humanism is a philosophy, belief system, or life stance that embraces human reason, logic, secular ethics, and philosophical naturalism, while specifically rejecting religious dogma, supernaturalism, and superstition as the basis of morality and decision making.

Secular humanism posits that human beings are capable of being ethical and moral without religion or belief in a deity. It does not, however, assume that humans are either inherently good or evil, nor does it present humans as being superior to nature. Rather, the humanist life stance emphasizes the unique responsibility facing humanity and the ethical consequences of human decisions. Fundamental to the concept of secular humanism is the strongly held viewpoint that ideology be it religious or political must be thoroughly examined by each individual and not simply accepted or rejected on faith. Along with this, an essential part of secular humanism is a continually adapting search for truth, primarily through science and philosophy.

I don't know if it is fair, or even possible, to label a show, movie, or series as "perfect", but this is the closest thing that I have ever seen to that in 50+ years! It represents the exact definition of story telling. From the way it is filmed, to the acting, to the handling of the subject matter, it grabs your attention, it pulls you in, and it doesn't let you go. It is so thought-provoking, without telling you what to think. It begs for understanding, without providing any answers. It allows you to LIVE through what must be the scariest, most difficult thing a child, or a parent, or a police officer, or a clinical psychologist can experience. You as a viewer don't observe it, you experience it. It is a master class on how to properly tell a story in a visual medium. The cameras merely document what is happening, in the most immersive but unobtrusive way possible. (There are no "action" sequences simulated by a shaky cam. There are no emotions triggered by manipulative soundtracks). All of it, every ounce of feeling, is generated by expert acting, set design, production planning, and execution. I used to consider "Glengarry Glen Ross" a master class in acting. "Adolescence" is a master class in nearly every component that makes a movie or series. Obviously, there is nothing ever created that appeals to EVERYONE, but if you appreciate creativity, proper story telling, thoughtful dialog, and masterful acting, then you will remember this one for a long time. Oh, and I forgot to mention that they accomplished all of this while filming each of the 4 one-hour episodes IN ONE TAKE, while using multiple locations AND a child actor who is performing in his first role! It is heart-wrenching and mind-blowing. Well done!

Why did they misrepresent information in the show?

Incel is an actual real movement, with a very specific ideology, that exists in its purest form on forums on weird corners of the internet banned off most social media. And it is popular.
The real ones actually condone violence against women, and have led to incidents of murder, and mass murder in the past.
They have what’s called a black-pill philosophy.
Their ideology is not comparable with what Andrew Tate says. The belief system and worldview are completely different, there not even remotely comparable.

My point is, this doesn’t help the problems. It creates more confusion about what exactly it is that young people get wrapped up in.
*So it widens the vacuum, don’t you understand?*
I assume they researched this enough before making the show to understand this is the case yet did so anyway. 
You can still disagree with Tate, that’s fine, but it is objectively not an incel ideology.
This only strengthens their sense of persecution.
If you want to challenge these narratives, that’s fine, but why not do a proper job of it by actually bringing the true contents of these spaces into full view? The material needs to be demystified and clarified, and separated out into its different components.

Lies might have a temporary effect, politically, but they don’t last. 
The truth is the best weapon to have on your side. Face the content fairly, and you will win far more supporters in the long run, because people are not stupid and they know when someone’s coming from the right place.

Even when they refer to the “manosphere”, that’s not a movement. It’s more like a category, used to describe spaces where men speak. There are many different movements that would fall under the banner of manosphere, many of which are delineated by virtue of the fact they don’t agree with eachother. Many are non-toxic and positive.
So when the boy in the show says “it’s a call to action by the manosphere”, that doesn’t make any sense. It’s fear mongering, because it makes it sound militaristic, when really it’s a xenophobic fantasy.

There is genuine “call to action” by real incels, that has happened in the past and has led to mass murders.
Obfuscation never helps a problem.
Misinformation widens the vacuum, which leaves gaps for people to fall into.
